segments.text: In the 1970s, crime was at an all-time
high in New York City. [music]
But what was surprising is by 1990s, the
crime [music] went down drastically and
nobody could understand how or why that
happened. What was found out is that
there were two changes that were made.
Number one, all graffiti was cleaned
[music] up and number two, the people
who would not pay to use the subways,
[music] they were punished. And only by
making these two changes, the crime went
down drastically from the 1970s to 1990s
[music]
in New York City. This is similar to the
broken windows theory, which basically
says that in any neighborhood, let's say
there's one broken window and if it is
left [music] unattended, soon another
broken window is going to appear and
then another and then there's [music]
going to be some spitting, then there's
going to be something else. Now, what
what it means is if you pass through a
neighborhood that has a lot of broken
windows and dirt and disorder, [music]
it is signaling to you that no one
cares. And if no one cares, that is an
invitation for more serious disorder
[music] and more serious crime. Now, why
am I telling you this? Well, because our
surroundings [music]
have a lot more influence on our
behavior than we realize. When you made
that mistake, the one that you're
beating yourself up about, your
environment was a much bigger player in
the event than you realize. So small
changes in your environment have [music]
big changes in your behavior.
